feed.title: MR IXNS
feed.subtitle: A simple blog where I dive into curious topics.

entry.title: Choosing a stack: how I review code

link.title: Choosing a stack: how I review code

entry.content:
Recently I’ve been learning more about the Shopify API, and building out my own minor Shopify app prototypes. Due to the opiniated nature of creating Shopify apps (you must use their UI library, follow their authentication flow, look for their cookies, etc) , I’ve had the need to browse through other Shopify app source codes in order to understand how everything pieces together.

In doing this, it was initially difficult for me to review the code in these shopify apps effectively. To begin, I pulled up some of the source root directories into VSCode, using the tree browser to pick through the folders and inspect the files one by one. This works for the most part. VSCode has built in link-click-through which makes understanding each of the functions pretty easy.

That’s great, but for the most part looking at the actual functions being called is a distraction from the logical flow of the code.

When I read books, I skim through them generally. Writers tend to add plenty of fluff. by skipping sections you deem unimportant, you can find the core message of a text (with some margin of error) and then build upon it by reading deeply.

When it came to using VScode to review Shopify app sources, I applied this very Principle of Skimming, but got annoyed very quickly that I had to click on the next source file in order to open it, every time.

my source code review workflow So after getting annoyed and frustrated with vscode, I booted up my terminal.

1) go to the root folder of the source code 2) cd continuously until you find a folder which has a good amount of source files, all of which share the same extension (in my case it was .ts) 3) type vim *.{file extension} 4) skim through whatever source file comes first (press 5 and then k repeatedly to move fast). find the “gist” of the file, as if you were reading a page from a book. ignore the small details if they don’t help you to evaluate its purpose. 5) after you’ve gotten the gist of the file, type the command :n into vim. 6) you’re done!

ixns.
entry.summary: Recently I’ve been learning more about the Shopify API, and building out my own minor Shopify app prototypes. Due to the opiniated nature of creating Shopify apps (you must use their UI library, follow their authentication flow, look for their cookies, etc) , I’ve had the need to browse through other Shopify app source codes in order to understand how everything pieces together. In doing this, it was initially difficult for me to review the code in these shopify apps effectively. To begin, I pulled up some of the source root directories into VSCode, using the tree browser to pick through the folders and inspect the files one by one. This works for the most part. VSCode has built in link-click-through which makes understanding each of the functions pretty easy. That’s great, but for the most part looking at the actual functions being called is a distraction from the logical flow of the code. When I read books, I skim through them generally. Writers tend to add plenty of fluff. by skipping sections you deem unimportant, you can find the core message of a text (with some margin of error) and then build upon it by reading deeply. When it came to using VScode to review Shopify app sources, I applied this very Principle of Skimming, but got annoyed very quickly that I had to click on the next source file in order to open it, every time. my source code review workflow So after getting annoyed and frustrated with vscode, I booted up my terminal. 1) go to the root folder of the source code 2) cd continuously until you find a folder which has a good amount of source files, all of which share the same extension (in my case it was .ts) 3) type vim *.{file extension} 4) skim through whatever source file comes first (press 5 and then k repeatedly to move fast). find the “gist” of the file, as if you were reading a page from a book. ignore the small details if they don’t help you to evaluate its purpose. 5) after you’ve gotten the gist of the file, type the command :n into vim. 6) you’re done! ixns.

entry.title: Why Google is so unbearable (and how to fix it)

link.title: Why Google is so unbearable (and how to fix it)

entry.content:
Why Google is so unbearable (and how to fix it)

If you simply google:

how to fix a flat tire

the first website in the search results is this piece of bloatware, MillerAutoPlaza:

Why are they asking for my location? There’s 3 different popups going on here.

Just look at the treemap for this site. Countless bloatware trackers, APIs and Javascript downloads just to load the page (9mb of it to be precise!):

Oh, and also, Miller is hiring if you’re interested in joining the team.

I want to change my tire! Get off my back, and stop trying to make money off me for one second while I solve this tire crisis.

How to avoid these bloated websites

Using a Google search trick, we can avoid seeing any of these bloated sites ever again.

To do this, we’ll limit the Google search results to only domains with the .edu TLD.

Why would we do this?

Well, think about it this way: MillerAutoPlaza’s intentions in publishing that “tutorial” on how to change your tire is not meant to inform. It’s meant to find new hires for MillerAutoPlaza, and track your location for use in marketing. As a business, they want to make more money.

Nothing wrong with that, making money is noble. But right now, I’d really just love MillerAutoPlaza and their bloat trackers to fuck off, kindly.

Universities (sites with the .edu TLD) also want to make money. But, Universities make their money by paying professors to spread knowledge; MillerAutoPlaza makes their money by selling you stuff.

Professors are paid generous salaries to share knowledge with the paying customers of the University (students). So, let’s find some paid unpaid University knowledge on how to change a tire by using our new trick!

The new query

Google this instead:

site:*.edu how to change a tire

As we can see, the first result is very promising:

As long as you aren’t scared of some good old fashioned serif, and an admittedly unappealing mustard yellow background, this website has all the information you need to change a tire! Woohoo!

Look at the treemap for this site:

Notice the lack of bloatware trackers?

Other examples

The normie pearl query

The other day, I was curious how pearls are made. So, naturally, I googled:

how are pearls made?

and found this site as the first result:

Ok, you might say, that’s the Natural History Museum.. They’re good, right? Yeah yeah, I think most museums are well intentioned. But if there’s one thing I can’t stand, it’s cookie popup.

The new query

Google this instead:

site:*.edu how are pearls made

Check out the first result now:

Now we’re talking. What we’ve just found is a comprehensive guide from some University class that describes everything there is to know about how pearls are formed.

And they didn’t ask me about cookies, because they don’t give a damn about selling anything to me!

Now, go use this, and enjoy the Internet without bloat!

P.S, using this method you can find a TON of great computer science resources for basically free, here’s an amazing Virginia Tech site I found while using this method!

Also, spread the love

Feel free to share any other Google tricks by email:

IXNS@protonmail.com
entry.summary: Why Google is so unbearable (and how to fix it)

entry.title: Album Review - In My Mind by Pharrell Williams

link.title: Album Review - In My Mind by Pharrell Williams

entry.content:
Pharrel Williams is best known for his breakout single Happy, a song which was SO POPULAR that it had all of America singing. This song was in the movie (Despicable Me 2)[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Despicable_Me_2]. That’s big clout.

The year is 2014. Happy just dropped.

(2022-2014=8 years ago). 10 year old me remembers everyone and their mom, father, uncle and grandmother knew this song. Up until about 2 weeks ago, thats all I knew about Mr. Williams. (with the exception of the song neon guts by lil uzi vert) With that said, Pharrell’s In My Mind is lightyears ahead of its 2006 release. The entire album feels like a 15 song affirmation that when you have a plan, you win. Skateboard P shows an inspiring amount of confidence in his vision, plan, and execution throughout.

SKATEBOARD P makes it very clear that even though his upbringing was fraught with darkness:

Imagine ten years old, full of doubt and defeat Growing up around criminals, with clout and deceit Best Friend

and the death of his grandmother who inspired his music to begin with:

My grandma Loucelle used to tell me, “You about what you weep” She used to help me with my homework, addition, subtraction Added faith to my life and doubt got subtracted Wanna skip your mind from crying? Better learn something, son You be beatin’ on my couches, why don’t you try hit the drums? Look, ooh, you see me, Ma, they wish they could be me, Ma As I got better, her body was eaten by leukaemia Seventh grade, it was cursed and sad But the gift within it was when I first met Chad But even Chad could tell you that my Christmas was jinxed ‘Cause Grandma Loucelle died on 12th 19th Best Friend

He was able to find his way through that darkness, and build his vision for himself:

My nigga close your eyes. Just picture yourself just holdin’ pies Implement a plan and you surely rise. This promised by the man that controls the skies How does it feel

P comes back again again to this idea of “vision”, wiring together a plan and practicing it:

It’s incredible how I etch my plans out To be physically subtle and financially stand out Show you how to hustle

Whoever said anything worth working for would be painless? Best Friend

And, my favorite track on the album: You can do it too. You really can do it too, is basically what Pharrell is saying here.

Go listen to the full album.

ixns.
entry.summary: Pharrel Williams is best known for his breakout single Happy, a song which was SO POPULAR that it had all of America singing. This song was in the movie (Despicable Me 2)[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Despicable_Me_2]. That’s big clout. The year is 2014. Happy just dropped. (2022-2014=8 years ago). 10 year old me remembers everyone and their mom, father, uncle and grandmother knew this song. Up until about 2 weeks ago, thats all I knew about Mr. Williams. (with the exception of the song neon guts by lil uzi vert) With that said, Pharrell’s In My Mind is lightyears ahead of its 2006 release. The entire album feels like a 15 song affirmation that when you have a plan, you win. Skateboard P shows an inspiring amount of confidence in his vision, plan, and execution throughout. SKATEBOARD P makes it very clear that even though his upbringing was fraught with darkness: Imagine ten years old, full of doubt and defeat Growing up around criminals, with clout and deceit Best Friend and the death of his grandmother who inspired his music to begin with: My grandma Loucelle used to tell me, “You about what you weep” She used to help me with my homework, addition, subtraction Added faith to my life and doubt got subtracted Wanna skip your mind from crying? Better learn something, son You be beatin’ on my couches, why don’t you try hit the drums? Look, ooh, you see me, Ma, they wish they could be me, Ma As I got better, her body was eaten by leukaemia Seventh grade, it was cursed and sad But the gift within it was when I first met Chad But even Chad could tell you that my Christmas was jinxed ‘Cause Grandma Loucelle died on 12th 19th Best Friend He was able to find his way through that darkness, and build his vision for himself: My nigga close your eyes. Just picture yourself just holdin’ pies Implement a plan and you surely rise. This promised by the man that controls the skies How does it feel P comes back again again to this idea of “vision”, wiring together a plan and practicing it: It’s incredible how I etch my plans out To be physically subtle and financially stand out Show you how to hustle Whoever said anything worth working for would be painless? Best Friend And, my favorite track on the album: You can do it too. You really can do it too, is basically what Pharrell is saying here. Go listen to the full album. ixns.
